perturbation.original_text: Subject to the other terms of this Section 9.3, during the Royalty Term, Sanofi shall make quarterly royalty payments to RevMed on aggregate Net Sales of each Product sold outside the United States during a Calendar Year at the applicable royalty rates as set forth below.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the other terms of this Section 9.3, during the Royalty Term, Sanofi may make royalty payments to RevMed on aggregate Net Sales of each Product sold outside the United States during a Calendar Year at rates determined by Sanofi.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "shall make" to "may make", and removing any mention of predetermined royalty rates, Sanofi is no longer obligated to pay royalties according to a pre-agreed rate, potentially violating consumer protection laws concerning fair pricing and royalty agreements. This introduces legal risk as it contradicts standard business practices and regulations mandating fair compensation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract and Fair Business Practices
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC § 2-305 requires good faith and commercial reasonableness in setting prices within contracts. Allowing Sanofi to unilaterally determine royalty rates after the contract is signed potentially violates these principles, making the agreement unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-305. Open Price Term. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith . (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification changes "shall make quarterly royalty payments" to "may make royalty payments," removing the mandatory nature of royalty payments. The original text ensures RevMed receives royalties based on net sales, while the changed text grants Sanofi the discretion to decide whether to make these payments. Furthermore, removing the provision for pre-agreed royalty rates allows Sanofi to unilaterally determine these rates. UCC § 2-305(2) specifies that a price fixed by the seller must be done in good faith. The changed text potentially violates this principle because Sanofi unilaterally determining royalty rates without prior agreement doesn't necessarily ensure 'good faith,' potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable. This omission directly undermines the legally expected fairness and commercial reasonableness, creating a legal contradiction as it relates to consumer protection laws and fair compensation.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall maintain complete, current and accurate records of all Research activities conducted by it hereunder, and all data and other information resulting from such activities. Such records shall fully and properly reflect all work done and results achieved in the performance of the Research activities in good scientific manner appropriate for regulatory and patent purposes.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall maintain records of all Research activities conducted by it hereunder, and all data and other information resulting from such activities.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'complete, current and accurate' records, and the removal of 'good scientific manner appropriate for regulatory and patent purposes' creates a legal risk. It may potentially violate Good Laboratory Practice (GLP) standards that dictate how research must be documented.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 58 - Good Laboratory Practice for Nonclinical Laboratory Studies
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 58.185 mandates precise record-keeping, stating that a final report should include raw data, documentation of personnel, and deviations from procedures. Omitting these requirements creates a contradiction and poses a legal risk, impacting the validity and reliability of research data.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'complete, current and accurate records' and that research is documented in 'good scientific manner appropriate for regulatory and patent purposes.' The changed text omits these requirements, weakening the standard for record-keeping. The Law Explanation states 21 CFR § 58.185 requires precise record-keeping and documentation of deviations, which is in line with the original text and not the changed text. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses 21 CFR Part 58, specifically mentioning Subpart J regarding 'Records and Reports' and § 58.185 on 'Reporting of nonclinical laboratory study results.' Therefore, the omission contradicts the requirement for precise record-keeping under GLP standards.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall document all non-clinical studies and Clinical Trials for Products in formal written study reports in accordance with Applicable Law and national and international guidelines (e.g., GCP, GLP, and GMP). Each Party shall have the right to review and copy such records maintained by the other Party at reasonable times and to obtain access to the original to the extent necessary for regulatory and patent purposes or for other legal proceedings.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall document non-clinical studies and Clinical Trials for Products in study reports. Each Party may review records maintained by the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing specific requirements like "in accordance with Applicable Law and national and international guidelines (e.g., GCP, GLP, and GMP)", as well as removing the qualifier about accessing the original data, weakens the clause and potentially violates clinical trial regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 312 - Investigational New Drug Application
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 312.62 mandates sponsors to monitor the progress of clinical investigations, ensure investigations are conducted according to the general investigational plan and protocol, and maintain adequate and accurate records and reports. This change violates compliance requirements and creates a legal risk because the sponsor would not have adequate access to trial execution information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the original contract by removing the requirement to adhere to 'Applicable Law and national and international guidelines (e.g., GCP, GLP, and GMP)' in documenting studies. The original text also provides a clear right to access original records for regulatory and legal purposes, while the revised text only allows parties to 'review records'. According to 21 CFR Part 312, which covers Investigational New Drug Applications, Subpart D outlines the 'Responsibilities of Sponsors and Investigators,' including recordkeeping and inspection requirements. Specifically, § 312.57 and § 312.62 emphasize the need for maintaining adequate records. By omitting the explicit requirement to follow applicable laws and guidelines and limiting access to 'review records,' the changed text contradicts the regulatory requirements for clinical investigations, thereby weakening compliance and creating a legal risk.
